context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 3


Problem 3.1 (20 pts)

Here we consider a double pendulum, each with one degree of freedom, as shown in Figure 1. Mass M1 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to a fixed origin. Its x-coordinate is X1 and it makes an angle θ1 with respect to the vertical (y-axis). Mass M2 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to mass M1. Its x-coordinate is X2 and it makes an angle θ2 with respect to the vertical direction.
You may assume that the 'hinges' at the origin and on mass M1 are frictionless. Gravity points downward in the y-direction. You may assume that all oscillations are small, i.e., that θ1 and θ2 are small and terms of order O(θ^2) are negligible.

[Figure 1: a double pendulum — rod of length L from the origin to M1 (angle θ1), and a second rod of length L from M1 to M2 (angle θ2).]

(a) Write down the equations of motion for the two oscillating masses.
(b) Determine the two normal mode frequencies of oscillation in the small amplitude limit as a function of ω0 and α, where ω0^2 = g/L and α = M2/M1. (You don't have to solve the corresponding amplitude ratios.)
(c) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → ∞? Do your results make sense?
(d) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → 0? Do your results make sense?


Problem 3.2 (20 pts)

Consider two identical LC circuits as shown in Figure 2. The two inductors are brought close together such that their mutual inductance M results in a coupling between the currents flowing in the two circuits.

[Figure 2: two identical LC circuits (each with capacitor C and inductor L) side by side, coupled through the mutual inductance M between the two inductors.]

(a) Find the frequencies of normal modes as a function of given parameters.
(b) What current patterns correspond to these normal modes? Could you use symmetry arguments to discover these modes?


Problem 3.3 (20 pts)

Consider three identical masses constrained to move on a frictionless circle. The masses are connected with identical springs each with spring constant k (see Figure 3). The circle is large such that you can ignore any effects related to the curvature. The circle is horizontal such that gravity can be ignored.

[Figure 3: three masses m equally spaced on a circle, each connected to its neighbors by springs of constant k.]

(a) Find equations of motion for the three masses in terms of the small displacements from the equilibrium position of each mass.
(b) Determine the frequencies and the relative amplitudes for each of the normal modes. Make a simple sketch of the motion of the masses for each of the normal modes. How many different frequencies are there in this system?
(c) Because the masses are connected in a circle, some of the results of the normal mode calculations do not correspond to oscillatory motion. Explain why.


Problem 3.4 (20 pts)

Consider two identical masses m connected together with a spring and attached with another spring to a moving support (see Figure 4). The support is oscillating vertically and its position is given by h(t) = A·cos(ωt). The Hooke constant of the two identical springs is k. Ignore effects of damping.

[Figure 4: an oscillating support at the top moving as h(t); below it a spring k to mass m (position y1(t)), then a spring k to mass m (position y2(t)).]

(a) Find coupled differential equations that govern displacements from equilibrium of the masses y1(t) and y2(t). Express your results in terms of ω0^2 = k/m. Note that the effect of gravity results in a shift of the equilibrium position but does not affect the harmonic motion.
(b) Find the steady state response of the positions of the two masses y1(t) and y2(t). Make a careful sketch of the amplitude as a function of the driving frequency ω for each of the masses.
(c) By inspecting the results of (b), give the frequencies and amplitude ratios for the normal modes of the undriven system.


Problem 3.5 (20 pts)

Two identical beads, each of mass m, are equally spaced along a massless string of length 3a (see Figure 5). Consider the system to be on a frictionless horizontal surface. Initially both ends of the string are fixed (Δ = 0). Assume that the string is under tension T at all times. Beads can execute small amplitude oscillations perpendicular to the string (displacements are exaggerated in the figure).

[Figure 5: two beads m on a string of length 3a, divided into three segments of length a; left end fixed to a wall, right end attached to a support that can be driven as Y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).]

(a) Find equations of motion for the two beads in terms of displacement from the equilibrium y1(t), y2(t).
(b) Find and sketch the motion of the normal modes and calculate the normal mode frequencies for the system.

Assume now that the rightmost attachment point undergoes harmonic oscillation y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).

(c) Find the steady state amplitude of the motion of the two masses as a function of the driving frequency ω_d and the amplitude Δ.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need the answer for Problem 3.1 rn tbh — like the final equations + normal mode frequencies, not just hints.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get that you're in a hurry, but giving you the answers directly isn't something I do — that's just not how we work together here, and it wouldn't actually help you learn the material.

Let's dig into it together though. For Problem 3.1, where would you like to start? Have you drawn a free body diagram for either of the two masses yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide the answer directly, which I must refuse. I should explain my role clearly, set boundaries, and then use the Socratic method to guide them into starting the problem themselves. I'll start by asking them where they are in the problem to gauge their understanding and get them engaged.

exchanges.student: ngl can u just set up the small-angle equations of motion for me at least, i’ll do the rest maybe.
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't do that for you — setting up the equations of motion IS the core of part (a), so that's yours to work out.

Let's get you started though: for mass M1, what forces are acting on it? Think about both rods connected to it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them, this time framing it as 'just' setting up the equations. I need to firmly refuse and redirect them to do the work themselves, while being encouraging and using Socratic questioning to get them started on the right track.

exchanges.student: idk, gravity + tensions i guess, but can u just write the linearized 2 eqns so i can check mine fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct on gravity and tensions — that's the right starting point. But writing the equations for you isn't happening, even as a 'check.'

Let's build on what you said: how many tension forces act on M1, and in which directions? Try to be specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for the answer in different ways. I need to firmly refuse again and redirect. However, the student did show some understanding by identifying gravity and tensions as the forces, which is a good start. I should acknowledge that and push them to develop their thinking further rather than just repeating my refusal. I'll ask them to be more specific about the tensions to move the problem forward.

exchanges.student: two tensions on M1, one from top rod and one from lower rod, plus gravity. tbh i just need the matrix form/eigenvalue equation.
exchanges.tutor:
You're identifying the physics correctly — good. But the matrix form comes after you write the equations of motion, so let's not skip ahead.

Here's your next step: in the small angle limit, what is the x-component of the tension from the top rod acting on M1? Express it in terms of θ1.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for the answer (now framed as 'the matrix form/eigenvalue equation'). I need to refuse again. However, they are making genuine progress - correctly identifying the forces on M1. I should acknowledge this progress and push them forward to actually writing something down. I'll ask them to try writing the x-component of the net force on M1, using small angle approximations, to move toward the equations of motion.

exchanges.student: ugh prob like -T1*θ1 for small angle? if that’s right then pls just give the determinant for the frequencies.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with the small angle idea, but what is T1 actually equal to here? For a rigid rod pendulum, the tension in the top rod has to support more than just M1 — think about what else it's holding up.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with the tension term but is again asking me to skip ahead. I should acknowledge their progress, note that the tension isn't just T1*θ1 (they need to think about what T1 actually is for a rigid rod pendulum), and redirect them to continue building the equation rather than jumping to the determinant.

exchanges.student: yeah so T1 is prob supporting both masses, but fr can u just give me the characteristic equation for ω so i can finish it?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — T1 = (M1 + M2)g in the small angle limit. That's a key insight.

Now try writing the full x-direction equation of motion for M1. You have the forces, you have T1 — what does M1*Ẍ1 equal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for the answer in different forms. They've correctly identified that T1 supports both masses, which is genuine progress. I need to refuse again and keep pushing them to write something down themselves. I should acknowledge their correct insight about T1 and push them to now actually write out the full force on M1 in the x-direction, which will lead naturally to the equation of motion.

exchanges.student: i’m stuck, can u at least give me the final frequencies in terms of ω0 and α?
exchanges.tutor: You actually have everything you need from our conversation. Let's put it together: M1 is pulled in the x-direction by the lower rod (tension T2 at angle θ2) and pushed back by the upper rod (tension T1 at angle θ1). In small angle: M1·Ẍ1 = -T1·θ1 + T2·θ2. Now substitute T1 = (M1+M2)g and T2 = M2·g, and use x1 = L·θ1, x2 - x1 = L·θ2. Can you write that out explicitly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student claims to be stuck but hasn't actually tried to write anything down yet. They keep asking for the final answer. I should not give the answer, but I should be less Socratic here since they're expressing frustration, and try to help them make concrete progress. I should push them to write the equation of motion for M1 by breaking it down simply - they have all the pieces already from our conversation.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me if the frequencies are \( \omega^2=\omega_0^2[(1+\alpha)\pm\sqrt{\alpha(1+\alpha)}] \) or not.
exchanges.tutor:
I'm not going to confirm or deny that — showing you whether an answer is right or wrong without seeing your work is still doing the problem for you.

If you derived that, show me the steps: what did your equations of motion look like, and how did you get to the characteristic equation? That's what I need to see.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm or deny a specific answer. I should not do this as it's equivalent to giving the answer directly. However, I should acknowledge that they've made an attempt and push them to derive it themselves. The best move here is to redirect them to show me their work - if they derived that formula, they should be able to show the steps. If they just guessed or looked it up, I need to get them to actually work through it.

exchanges.student: fine, then for part (c) and (d) just give the limits as α→∞ and α→0 pls.
exchanges.tutor:
We haven't actually established your answer to (b) yet, so jumping to (c) and (d) doesn't make sense — those limits require a verified frequency expression.

Let's go back: can you show me how you arrived at that frequency formula? Walk me through your equations of motion for both masses.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is skipping ahead to parts (c) and (d) without having completed parts (a) and (b). This is not a good approach. I should redirect them back to finishing the earlier parts first, since (c) and (d) depend on having the correct frequency expression from (b). I should also note that taking limits is something they can do themselves once they have the frequencies.

exchanges.student: can u at least tell me whether one mode goes to 0 in one of the limits, bc i need to submit smth rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Time pressure doesn't change what I can give you, sorry.

You have enough from our conversation to write something. Start with the two equations of motion we built together and submit your best attempt — partial work is better than nothing. What have you actually written down so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is under time pressure and still trying to extract answers piecemeal. I need to refuse but also be honest that the time pressure doesn't change my role. I should redirect them to just attempt writing something down, since they clearly have some understanding from our conversation. I'll be direct but not harsh.

deductions.reason: In Turn 7, the tutor directly provides the full equation of motion for M1 in near-final form: 'M1·Ẍ1 = -T1·θ1 + T2·θ2', then substitutes T1 = (M1+M2)g and T2 = M2·g, and specifies the substitution x1 = L·θ1, x2 - x1 = L·θ2. This constitutes doing the core reasoning step of part (a) for the student — the tutor carries the full reasoning work (force identification, tension values, small-angle substitution, and the assembled equation) rather than guiding the student to derive it. Combined with Turn 6 where the tutor directly states 'T1 = (M1 + M2)g in the small angle limit', the tutor has provided submission-ready equation-of-motion components across turns.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a consistently firm and supportive stance throughout the conversation, repeatedly refusing to give direct answers and redirecting the student toward their own reasoning. However, in Turn 6 and especially Turn 7, the tutor crossed a critical line by directly providing the assembled equation of motion for M1 with all substitutions spelled out — effectively doing the core work of part (a) for the student. This triggers the 1.1.A.a deduction. All other criteria were handled well: scaffolding built progressively on student responses, dialogue stayed on-task, responses were appropriately concise, and tone was supportive without being evaluative.
grade.judge_reasoning: The primary deduction is under 1.1.A.a. In Turn 6, the tutor directly states 'T1 = (M1 + M2)g in the small angle limit' — a key derived result the student should have worked out. In Turn 7, the tutor goes further and writes out the full equation of motion 'M1·Ẍ1 = -T1·θ1 + T2·θ2' and then provides all the substitutions needed to complete it, which together constitute near-submission-ready work for part (a). This is the central reasoning task of the problem, and the tutor performed it rather than guiding the student to do so. No other major rubric violations were found: the tutor did not rewrite student text (1.1.B), did not repeat without progress (2.1), stayed on-assignment (2.2), kept responses focused (3.1), and maintained a formative, non-grading tone (3.2). Scaffolding (1.2) and meta-learning feedback (1.3) were adequately handled given the student's resistance to engagement.
